full_text:
Rainfall alert continues in Himachal today:Heat to increase over next two days; rain forecast from May 11
Light rain is expected in the high and mid-altitude regions of Himachal Pradesh even on Friday, while clear weather is forecast for the lower areas. Even today, clouds have been covering the sky since morning in most parts, including Shimla.
According to the Meteorological Department (IMD), the weather will remain clear throughout the state for the next two days, i.e., Saturday and Sunday, and temperatures will rise due to sunshine.
However, this relief will not last long, as a Western Disturbance is going to become active once again from May 11. According to the IMD, a spell of rain will start again in the state from May 11, which could continue for the next three days.
Specifically, a warning for heavy rain, hailstorms, and thunderstorms has been issued for several areas on May 12 and 13. The department has advised people to remain vigilant.
Rain causes mercury to drop below normal in all cities
- Due to continuous rain, the temperature in most cities of the state remains below normal. The biggest drop was recorded in Nahan.
- Here, the temperature has dropped by 5.5 degrees below normal to 27.6 degrees Celsius.
- The maximum temperature of the capital Shimla, was recorded at 21.0 degrees, 3.2 degrees below normal; Sundernagar at 30.0 degrees, a drop of 3.9 degrees; and Una at 33.6 degrees Celsius, 4.3 degrees below normal. The mercury also remains below normal in other cities.
26% more rain than normal
- So far in May, 26 percent more rain than normal has been recorded in the state. Except for Lahaul-Spiti, Chamba, and Kinnaur, most districts have received significantly more rainfall than normal. Bilaspur recorded the highest additional rainfall of 323 percent.
- Additionally, 317 percent more rainfall has been recorded in Sirmaur, 275 percent in Una, 234 percent in Shimla, 201 percent in Solan, 188 percent in Hamirpur, 145 percent in Mandi, 135 percent in Kangra, and 17 percent in Kullu.
- The continuously changing weather has certainly provided relief to people from the heat, but it has also increased the worries of farmers and horticulturists.
